Wattle Bark and Black Wattle “Bark xtract.. The Company's reputation is established on the consistently good quality-of their products, which are marketed under -the registered name-"*BLEPHANT BRAND. a csyvmbol which is recognised by consumers -as ‘a -hall-mark of quahty. At the Company's extract factories 90;000 tons of green bark ‘can be converted annually into extract: © This extract is~universally ‘accepted by « (\, leading tanneries as being one of the finest obtainable and is ready for usein-its-pure’state without the additions of chemicals or other adulterating substances, E LE P H A NT B RA N D Export bark in pressed or bagged form ts prepared at the Company's SOLID BLACK WATTLEtour depots where every care is taken to maintain “ELEPHANT” quality, both in-the bark and the packing. Ltd.25 x Nso N ao N { i N8 J T -is with sincere pleasure ‘that I wvité a foreword for the South A frican Indian Who's Who-and Commercial Directory which has been compiled by Mr. Dhanee Bramdaw of Pietermaritzburg. Myo Bramdaw is not only a-valued personal friend, but isin my opinion one of thé ablest of the younger genération of Indians in South Africa. Genius has been defined by a well-known writer as an-infinite capacity for taking pains. If this tsa true definition, Mr. Bramdaw has Senius,; for to my knowledge he has spent much time and taken greal pains over the production of this directory. Mr. Bramdate's compilation, whose artistié value is enhanced by a large nimber of photographs, should be of great value not only to persons engaged in business but to the South Afritaw world al large; both European and Indian, and also to the Union Government, since it shows in a-compact form the important part which the Indian-has played in the progress and development of this sub-continent, A glance at the book will-also make clear, what is often not sufficiently ap preciated by Europeans, that the cast majority of Indians in ihis’ country have been born in South Africa and know no-other home..-The Government of India and the increasing number of people in India who take an intérest in-the welfare of their fellow-coitntrymen overseas will also find much io interest them in-these pages. I conimend this inost itseful compilation to the public of South Africa and India in the hope that time will bring these two great countries into closer and more friendly relations with each other. OCCUPATIONS OF THE INDIAN POPULATION OF THE UNION.(ii.) Occupations of Indians,—It is not passible to publish comparable figures in respect of occupations at the Censuses of 1911 and 1921. owing to a different classification having been adopted at the latter Census. Moreover, in 1911 the tabulation included the whole Indian community, whereas in 1921 tabulation was confined to persons of fifteen years of ageand over. The following tablegives the numbers of the Indian population of the Union of fifteen years of age and over, classified according to their occupation ag returned at the Census of 1921j .
